event: Scene of exile or lamentation, possibly the Babylonian captivity
symbols: angel observing from above — divine witness to human suffering and God's hidden presence even in exile
massive stone blocks and ruins — the destruction of the Temple or city, the collapse of the old order
seated weeping women — the mourners of Zion, the Daughter of Jerusalem in desolation
children among the exiles — the vulnerability of the innocent and the continuation of the covenant people
solitary man turning away at right — departure, exile, or the burden of leadership in suffering
description: A somber scene of lamentation and exile. At left, a winged angel sits atop a rocky outcrop near trees, gazing down at the scene below. A group of women in flowing colorful garments -- blue, gold, pink, white -- sit together in attitudes of grief, some embracing children. A nude or lightly clad child plays on the ground before them. At the right, a muscular man in a brown cloak leans or turns away beside massive stone blocks, possibly ruins or the entrance to a fortification. The background shows a hazy, barren landscape.
theological_description: This scene evokes the grief of the Babylonian exile, one of the defining traumas of Israel's history. The weeping women recall the 'Daughter of Zion' mourning in Lamentations, and the Psalmist's cry, 'By the waters of Babylon, there we sat down and wept, when we remembered Zion' (Psalm 137:1). The presence of children among the mourners underscores the intergenerational scope of the catastrophe and the prophetic concern for the innocent who suffer in national judgment. The angel watching from above is a profound theological detail: even in the darkest hour of exile, when it seemed God had abandoned His people, divine presence remained as witness and eventual deliverer. Jeremiah had promised that the exile would not be permanent -- 'I will restore your fortunes and gather you from all the nations' (Jeremiah 29:14). The massive stone ruins may represent the destroyed Temple or the walls of Jerusalem, whose fall Jeremiah mourned. The solitary man turning away could represent a prophet or leader bearing the weight of communal grief, echoing Jeremiah's identity as the 'weeping prophet' (Jeremiah 9:1).

scripture_refs.reason: By the waters of Babylon we sat and wept -- the scene of exile and lamentation directly depicted.
How lonely sits the city that was full of people -- the Daughter of Zion weeping with none to comfort her.
Jeremiah's wish that his eyes were a fountain of tears to weep for the slain of his people -- the prophetic grief embodied.
God's promise to restore the exiles after seventy years -- the angel's presence hints at this future hope.
Children fainting in the streets and crying to their mothers -- the vulnerable innocents in the scene of desolation.
